case_name: Balancing Mevinox and cyanide removal from surface water for groundwater recharge under moderate budget and limited operator skill
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for surface water contaminated with Mevinphos and cyanide intended for groundwater recharge, balancing multi-contaminant removal, cost, operator simplicity, and residuals handling.

input_data.scenario_background: A river diversion basin is being considered as a managed aquifer recharge source after an upstream pesticide handling incident involving Mevinox and intermittent discharge from a metal-finishing area contributing cyanide. The utility must achieve a recharge-compatible effluent with reliable removal of both an organophosphate pesticide and dissolved cyanide, but the site has only moderately trained operators and cannot support a highly sophisticated treatment train. Several technically viable routes exist, including oxidation-based destruction and adsorption or membrane polishing, but the preferred option must balance compliance confidence against cost, operating complexity, and manageable residuals.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated water should be suitable for groundwater recharge with strong reduction of Mevinphos and cyanide to low and stable levels, while avoiding treatment byproducts or residuals that could compromise subsurface infiltration or permit compliance.
secondary_goals: maintain stable performance during moderate surface-water quality fluctuation
keep life-cycle cost within a moderate municipal budget

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer residual streams that can be dewatered and disposed conventionally; avoid difficult high-strength concentrate or highly hazardous spent regenerant handling
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system should tolerate seasonal turbidity and DOC swings without requiring highly specialized optimization

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended solids and part of the organic load to stabilize downstream oxidation and adsorption
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide low-turbidity water and protect oxidation and GAC units
core_unit_functions.alkaline_chlorination_oxidation: oxidize free cyanide to less toxic products under controlled pH while also degrading Mevinphos
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_granular_activated_c: polish residual pesticide and oxidation byproducts and improve recharge-water organic quality

applicability_rationale: this train directly addresses both contaminants using a practical combination of destruction and polishing rather than relying on one mechanism alone
cyanide is commonly treated by alkaline chlorination, and pH-controlled oxidation can be implemented with moderate operator skill if instrumentation and SOPs are in place
pretreatment lowers turbidity and organic demand, improving oxidant efficiency and reducing variability in performance
BAC or GAC polishing helps manage residual Mevinphos fragments and other oxidizable organics in surface water intended for recharge
overall, the process balances compliance confidence with moderate cost and avoids the concentrate disposal burden of membrane systems
potential_risks: cyanide oxidation requires strict pH control to avoid unsafe conditions and incomplete destruction
chlorination of cyanide and natural organic matter can create byproducts if oxidant dose and quench are poorly managed
GAC replacement frequency may increase if DOC spikes seasonally
sludge from clarification will require routine handling

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce solids and part of DOC to improve downstream oxidation performance
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: produce clarified water for ozone contact and biofiltration
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize Mevinphos and contribute to cyanide destruction under controlled conditions
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration: biodegrade ozonation byproducts and improve biodegradable organic removal
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: provide adsorption backup and recharge-water polishing

applicability_rationale: this route is technically attractive for broad organic contaminant control and can handle the pesticide component well
ozone followed by biologically active filtration is a recognized approach for micropollutant transformation and polishing in surface waters
it can reduce reliance on heavy chlorination and may offer lower chlorinated byproduct concern in some matrices
however, cyanide treatment with ozone is more sensitive to dose, contact, and water matrix than the alkaline chlorination route, so it ranks below the top option for a limited-skill site
potential_risks: cyanide destruction may be less straightforward to verify than with dedicated alkaline chlorination control
ozone systems require more specialized maintenance and safety management
bromate or other oxidation byproducts may need evaluation depending on source-water bromide
biofilters need careful startup and stable operation

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove settleable solids and reduce particulate-associated organic load
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect oxidation and adsorption units from solids carryover
core_unit_functions.alkaline_chlorination_oxidation: destroy cyanide and oxidize part of the Mevinphos load
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual pesticide and improve final organics for recharge

applicability_rationale: this is a simpler variant of the top-ranked train and remains highly feasible for the target contaminants
the reduced process count may appeal to the operator-skill constraint and moderate budget
it is a reasonable choice where oxidation control is well established and biological activity in GAC is not intentionally managed
it ranks slightly lower because non-biological GAC alone may provide less resilient polishing of oxidation byproducts and variable DOC than BAC-oriented operation
potential_risks: higher reliance on fresh GAC adsorption capacity when DOC increases
less byproduct biodegradation than biologically active polishing
media exhaustion and breakthrough need monitoring
continued need for controlled cyanide oxidation chemistry

core_unit_functions.media_filtration: protect membrane elements from turbidity and particulates
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong barrier removal for dissolved contaminants including cyanide species and many organic molecules
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_or_gac_post_polish: polish permeate or address residual trace organics if needed
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry for recharge compatibility

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment is technically capable and offers a high-confidence physical barrier for multiple dissolved contaminants
it may be attractive if future source-water deterioration introduces additional salts or dissolved contaminants
despite technical strength, this route is downgraded because it creates a concentrate stream containing cyanide and pesticide residues that is difficult to manage
higher energy use, more operator attention, and fouling sensitivity make it a poor fit for the stated constraints
potential_risks: concentrate disposal may be the controlling implementation problem
surface-water fouling potential increases pretreatment burden and cleaning frequency
skilled membrane operation and integrity management are needed
permeate remineralization or stabilization may be required before recharge

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove suspended matter and lower solids loading
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect downstream resin and carbon beds
core_unit_functions.anion_exchange_or_specialty_resin: capture cyanide species or related complexes depending on water chemistry and resin selection
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb Mevinphos and provide final polishing

applicability_rationale: adsorption and ion exchange create a non-destructive treatment route that may appear simpler on paper
GAC is plausible for organophosphate pesticide removal, and specialty resins can address some cyanide forms
however, performance for cyanide is highly chemistry-dependent and spent resin or regenerant management can become difficult
because the site prefers limited operator burden and manageable residuals, this route is less aligned than oxidation-based destruction followed by polishing
potential_risks: cyanide speciation and competing anions can reduce resin effectiveness
regeneration chemicals or spent resin may create hazardous residual handling challenges
GAC alone does not solve cyanide destruction
more frequent changeout may be needed during DOC or contaminant spikes

preferred_rank_pattern: oxidation-based routes that explicitly address cyanide should rank near the top
surface-water pretreatment should appear ahead of polishing units because turbidity and DOC affect downstream performance
membrane treatment may appear as a technically strong but lower-ranked option due to concentrate and complexity burdens
an adsorption-only or resin-heavy route should not rank first because cyanide destruction and residual handling are key trade-offs

must_reflect: need to remove both Mevinphos and cyanide rather than optimizing for only one contaminant
moderate budget that allows conventional pretreatment plus targeted advanced steps but disfavors highly expensive systems
limited operator skill that penalizes processes requiring intensive tuning, hazardous regenerant handling, or complex membrane operation
groundwater recharge context requiring attention to low residual oxidant, stable organics control, and manageable byproducts

must_contain: why cyanide-specific oxidation control is important and can dominate ranking
why surface-water turbidity and DOC make pretreatment relevant before oxidation, adsorption, or membranes
why GAC or BAC is useful as polishing for Mevinphos and oxidation byproducts but is not sufficient by itself for cyanide management
why technically capable membrane options are downgraded by concentrate disposal and operator burden

should_mention: pH control and safety considerations during cyanide oxidation
formation and management of oxidation byproducts or excess oxidant
GAC breakthrough or shortened media life under variable DOC loading
residuals from clarification, spent media, regenerant, or membrane concentrate
